cta.title: Call to action
cta.summary: Centered headline + description with up to two action buttons. High-emphasis section.
cta.whenToUse: Use as a closing section on landing pages, or mid-page when the goal is to drive a single decision. Prefer the hero block for the top-of-page hook.

examples.title: Ready to ship faster?
examples.description: Get a marketing site live in minutes — no code, no templates to fight.

examples.title: Last chance to lock in launch pricing.

text.title: Text / prose
text.summary: Markdown-driven prose block with optional heading + eyebrow. The general-purpose narrative section.
text.whenToUse: Use for any explanatory text — about copy, methodology, longer-form content embedded inside a marketing page. For full blog posts use the post pipeline; for short statements prefer hero or cta.

examples.title: About supa.page
examples.body: supa.page lets you ship marketing sites by talking to Claude. **No visual editor. No templates to fight.**

examples.title: How the rankings work
examples.body:
1. We pull data from the official sources.
2. We normalise units.
3. We publish weekly.

See the [full methodology](/methodology).

faq.title: FAQ
faq.summary: Disclosure-based Q&A. Auto-emits FAQPage JSON-LD for AI search citations.
faq.whenToUse: Use to surface 3–12 common questions. For long-form support content use a dedicated docs site, not an FAQ section.

examples.title: Frequently asked questions

items.question: Can I use a custom domain?
items.answer: Yes — every paid plan includes one custom domain.

items.question: Do I need to write code?
items.answer: No. Claude writes the site for you in plain language.

feature-grid.title: Feature grid
feature-grid.summary: 2-, 3-, or 4-column grid of features with optional icon, description, and per-item CTA.
feature-grid.whenToUse: Use to break down a product into 3–9 atomic features. For deeper differentiation use comparison or stats. For 1–2 items, use hero + a single secondary section instead.

examples.title: Why teams pick supa.page

items.title: Talk, ship, iterate
items.description: Tell Claude what changed and the site updates.

items.title: Your own domain
items.description: Custom domain with TLS on every paid plan.

items.title: No visual editor
items.description: No templates to fight. Plain language.

examples.title: Three steps

items.title: Describe
items.description: Tell Claude what site you want.

items.title: Iterate
items.description: Ask for changes in plain language.

items.title: Publish
items.description: Live on your domain in seconds.

cookie-banner.title: Cookie consent banner
cookie-banner.summary: EU/UK-compliant consent banner. Persists choice in localStorage; gates analytics on accept.
cookie-banner.whenToUse: Use on every public marketing site that runs analytics or tracking, regardless of jurisdiction. The banner self-dismisses once a choice is persisted.

message.default: We use cookies to measure traffic on this site.

examples.message: We use cookies to measure traffic on this site.
This site uses analytics cookies to understand how you read.

newsletter-form.title: Newsletter signup form
newsletter-form.summary: Single-input email capture with AJAX submit + success swap. Falls back to a native form post.
newsletter-form.whenToUse: Use to collect email subscribers on landing pages, blog footers, or above the fold. For full lead-capture (name, company, message), use a dedicated contact-form block instead.

examples.title: Get weekly updates
examples.description: New posts, no spam. Unsubscribe anytime.
examples.success_message: You're in. Look out for the next issue.

examples.title: Join 1,200 builders

hero-centered.title: Hero — centered
hero-centered.summary: Above-the-fold centered hero. Optional badge, h1 headline, description, up to two CTAs, optional microcopy line.
hero-centered.whenToUse: Use as the first section on a landing or product page. The single most-edited block — every site has one. Prefer this variant for product pitches, marketing pages, and announcement-driven launches. For asymmetric layouts use hero-split-image; for stat-heavy hooks use hero-stats.

examples.title: The fastest way to ship marketing pages your customers actually read.
examples.description: Atlas turns plain JSON into production-grade marketing sites. No build step, no framework lock-in.
examples.microcopy: Free for 14 days · No credit card required

examples.title: Build it in plain English. Ship it on your domain.
examples.description: Describe what you want. Claude builds. You publish.

pricing-cards-3.title: Pricing — 3 cards
pricing-cards-3.summary: Three pricing tiers side-by-side with optional featured highlight + floating "Most popular" badge.
pricing-cards-3.whenToUse: Use for SaaS pricing pages with exactly 3 tiers (Starter / Team / Business is the canonical shape). For 1-2 tiers prefer pricing-simple; for tier comparison matrices use pricing-table.

examples.title: Honest plans. No salesperson required.
examples.description: Annual billing saves 20%. All plans include unlimited bandwidth.

tiers.name: Starter
tiers.description: For solo developers shipping side projects.

tiers.name: Team
tiers.description: For startups graduating from a landing-page builder.

tiers.name: Business
tiers.description: For marketing teams running real revenue traffic.

testimonials-grid-3.title: Testimonials — 3 cards
testimonials-grid-3.summary: Three customer quotes side-by-side. Each card has optional 5-star rating, blockquote, avatar + name + role.
testimonials-grid-3.whenToUse: Use for social-proof sections with 3 named, on-page testimonials. Anonymous quotes should NOT be marked up — they break the Google self-serving-reviews policy guard in the future schema emission. For more quotes use testimonials-masonry; for one hero quote use testimonial-quote-big.

examples.title: Real teams shipping real pages.

items.quote: Cut our marketing-site bug reports by 70%. The schema validation catches everything before production.
items.author: Lior Shafir

items.quote: I'm a designer. I shipped a 14-page site without opening a code editor. The result looks like our brand.
items.author: Anika Patel

items.quote: The IndexNow integration started showing up in ChatGPT answers within four days.
items.author: Yusuf Karim

stats-row-4.title: Stats — 4-up row
stats-row-4.summary: Four pre-formatted stats in a divided row. No structured data emission per the design-system spec.
stats-row-4.whenToUse: Use for at-a-glance metrics (uptime, customers, performance numbers). For numbers needing context use stats-with-context; for narrative-led numbers fold them into the page description prose instead.

logos-row.title: Logos — single row
logos-row.summary: Single-row customer logo cloud. Each item is an image OR a styled wordmark. Emits no structured data.
logos-row.whenToUse: Use to show "Trusted by" or "As featured in" rows. For framed multi-row grids use logos-grid. Keep to 4-10 items — beyond 10 the row gets too dense; switch to logos-grid.

announcement-bar.title: Announcement bar
announcement-bar.summary: Top-of-page strip with badge, message, optional promo code, CTA link, and dismissible X button. Persists choice.
announcement-bar.whenToUse: Use for time-sensitive announcements at the top of every page: product launches, sales, status updates. Rotate the `id` field when the announcement content changes — that re-shows the bar to users who dismissed the previous one.

examples.message: Atlas v3 is now generally available — get 30% off year one

cta-banner.title: CTA — banner
cta-banner.summary: Single-row accent banner with title + description on the left, large CTA on the right.
cta-banner.whenToUse: Use as a mid-page or footer-adjacent conversion strip when you want a tight, single-action prompt. For above-the-fold use hero-centered; for closing conversion blocks use cta-centered.

examples.title: Atlas v3 is out. Migrate by Aug 1 for 30% off year one.
examples.description: Existing v2 customers get the upgrade free. New customers get the discount.

examples.title: Ready to ship faster?

features-grid-6.title: Features — 6-up grid
features-grid-6.summary: Six features in a 3×2 divided grid. Each cell has optional inline icon + h3 title + short body.
features-grid-6.whenToUse: Use when you have exactly 6 capabilities to surface. For 3-4 use features-grid-3; for asymmetric mix use features-bento; for paragraph-rich features use features-alt.

examples.title: Capability sprawl, organised.

items.title: Edge-first
items.body: Pinned isolates per POP. First invocation in 3ms.

items.title: Audit by default
items.body: Every edit is logged with author, timestamp, and diff.

items.title: Custom domain
items.body: Auto-TLS, IPv6, HTTP/3. Apex domains supported.

items.title: Typed content
items.body: Zod schemas validate every block before publish.

items.title: Open source primitives
items.body: tokens.css + primitives.css are MIT. Fork them.

team-grid.title: Team — 4-up grid
team-grid.summary: Team members in a 4-column grid. Each member has a photo OR initials avatar, plus name and role.
team-grid.whenToUse: Use on About / Team pages with 4-12 members. For leadership-only lists with social links use team-list. Schema contributes to Organization.employee[] in the page-level @graph (per the SEO/GEO plan).

examples.title: A small team of repeat builders.
examples.description: Twelve humans across Lisbon, Brooklyn, and Lagos.

nav-simple.title: Navigation — simple header
nav-simple.summary: Page header with brand wordmark + top-level nav links + optional login/CTA buttons.
nav-simple.whenToUse: Use as the page header on most marketing sites. For dropdown menus + search use nav-mega. The page renderer should treat nav blocks as page-level chrome (rendered once at the top) once chrome handling lands in A4.

footer-simple.title: Footer — simple
footer-simple.summary: Page footer with brand wordmark + copyright, link list, and optional social icon links.
footer-simple.whenToUse: Use as the page footer on most marketing sites. Single-row layout that wraps on narrow viewports. For a multi-column footer with grouped link columns, use footer-columns (not yet shipped).

hero-split-image.title: Hero — split with image
hero-split-image.summary: Asymmetric two-column hero. Eyebrow + h1 + description + two CTAs on the left, feature image on the right.
hero-split-image.whenToUse: Use as the first section on product, launch, or marketing pages where a feature image, screenshot, or hero shot meaningfully supports the headline. Prefer over hero-centered when the visual is the proof. For pure text above-the-fold use hero-centered; for stat-led openings use hero-stats.

examples.title: Auth that disappears into your product.
examples.description: Drop-in components, SSO on every plan tier, audit trails by default. Built by the same team behind the Stripe of identity.

image.alt: Authentication dashboard showing live session activity and SSO connections.

examples.title: The fastest path from a CSV to a forecast.
examples.description: Upload anything tabular. Atlas builds the model, the dashboard, and the alerts.

image.alt: Forecasting dashboard with quarterly revenue projections.

hero-stats.title: Hero — with stats
hero-stats.summary: Centered hero with eyebrow, h1, description, up to two CTAs, plus a 3-stat row that doubles as social proof.
hero-stats.whenToUse: Use as the first section when the pitch leans on concrete numbers (ARR, customer count, uptime, throughput). Prefer over hero-centered when proof points are load-bearing. For pure benefit-led copy use hero-centered; for standalone metrics deeper on the page use stats-row-4.

examples.eyebrow: Trusted by builders shipping every day
examples.title: The fastest way to ship marketing pages your customers actually read.
examples.description: Atlas turns plain JSON into production-grade marketing sites. Edge-rendered, no build step, no framework lock-in.

examples.title: The CDN that keeps 99.99% of your customers happy.
examples.description: Edge compute, image optimisation, and instant cache invalidation — shipped from 280+ POPs.

hero-minimal.title: Hero — minimal
hero-minimal.summary: Stripped-down hero. Small eyebrow, h1, one description sentence, one CTA. Left-aligned, narrow column.
hero-minimal.whenToUse: Use when content matters more than decoration — editorial homepages, manifestos, indie projects, personal sites, anything that wants quiet confidence. Prefer hero-centered for product launches with badges and dual CTAs; prefer hero-split-image when a visual carries the pitch.

examples.title: A magazine for people who still read.
examples.description: Weekly essays, no scroll-jacking, no clickbait. Hand-set by an editor who used to work at Harper’s.

examples.title: I write software, mostly for myself.
examples.description: Notes on small tools, side projects, and the slow craft of building things that last.

pricing-simple.title: Pricing — single tier
pricing-simple.summary: Centred single-tier pricing card with name, price, blurb, feature list, and one CTA.
pricing-simple.whenToUse: Use for solo products / one-plan SaaS / lifetime deals / one-off purchases where there is exactly one offering to sell. For 3 tiers use pricing-cards-3; for comparison matrices use pricing-table.

examples.title: One plan. Everything included.
examples.description: No tiers, no upsells. Cancel any time.
examples.blurb: Everything you need to ship a marketing site this afternoon.
features: Edit via Claude or the dashboard

examples.title: The book, in print and PDF.
examples.description: 318 pages, 16 case studies, every figure typeset in InDesign.
examples.blurb: Ships from Brooklyn in two weeks. Free updates forever.

testimonial-quote-big.title: Testimonial — big quote
testimonial-quote-big.summary: Single oversized pull-quote, centered. Large serif italic blockquote with avatar + name + role beneath.
testimonial-quote-big.whenToUse: Use for ONE hero testimonial that should dominate a section — a quote pulled from a customer interview or Slack (with permission). For 3 named quotes side-by-side use testimonials-grid-3. Anonymous quotes should NOT be marked up — they break the Google self-serving-reviews policy guard in the future schema emission.

examples.quote: I've tried every flat-file CMS in the last decade. This is the first one whose JSON I'd hand to a non-developer and trust them not to break the site.
examples.author: Dario Vega

examples.quote: We replaced four tools and a part-time developer with supa.page. Six weeks in, we ship more, not less, and the marketing team owns the site end-to-end.
examples.author: Reema Gupta

stats-with-context.title: Stats — with context
stats-with-context.summary: Two-column block: left has eyebrow + h2 + description + optional ghost link; right has a 2×2 grid of accent-coloured stats.
stats-with-context.whenToUse: Use when numbers need narrative framing — annual recap, milestone post, "by the numbers" deep-cut. For a stand-alone divided row of metrics use stats-row-4. For metrics that headline the page use hero-stats.

examples.title: The first year, summarised.
examples.description: We started shipping in February 2025 with seven beta customers. Twelve months later, here's where we are — and where we're going.

examples.title: Built to stay up while you sleep.
examples.description: Edge-rendered pages, automatic image optimisation, and instant cache invalidation across 280+ POPs — measured every minute, reported quarterly.

steps-numbered.title: Steps — numbered row
steps-numbered.summary: 3–6 numbered circular badges in a horizontal row connected by a thin line. Each step has a title and short body.
steps-numbered.whenToUse: Use for process / how-it-works narratives with a strict, ordered sequence (3–6 steps). For unordered capabilities use features-grid-3 or features-grid-6; for longer narrative steps with imagery use steps-stacked.

examples.title: From signup to live site in four steps.

steps.title: Pick a theme
steps.body: Choose from 14 token-driven themes, or start from blank.

steps.title: Connect content
steps.body: Import from Notion, Markdown, or your existing site.

steps.title: Preview locally
steps.body: See changes in real time. Share preview URLs with your team.

steps.title: Ship to prod
steps.body: Deploy to your domain. CDN propagation in 1.2 seconds.

examples.title: Three steps to your first deploy.
examples.description: No credit card, no template wrestling. Just describe and ship.

steps.title: Describe
steps.body: Tell Claude what site you want in plain language.

steps.title: Iterate
steps.body: Refine sections, copy, and theme by chatting.

steps.title: Publish
steps.body: One command pushes live to your custom domain.
